title: Recognizing the contributions of the Charles B. Rangel Graduate Fellowship Program, the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program, the William D. Clarke, Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship, and the Donald M. Payne International Development Graduate Fellowship Program to advance America's national security, development, and diplomacy efforts.
plain_summary: This resolution recognizes four fellowship programs that help bring more diverse people into important jobs in American foreign affairs. These programs help people from minority backgrounds and those who need financial help get jobs at the State Department and USAID. The goal is to have people from all different backgrounds working on America's relationships with other countries. This helps make America's diplomacy and national security stronger by including many different kinds of talented people.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Whereas Clauses
sections.text: The Department of State, the United States Agency for International Development (USAID), and other foreign affairs agencies need workers with different talents, skills, and experiences. This helps them protect American citizens living in other countries, create business opportunities for American companies, and carry out America's foreign policy. Congress has required by law that the Department of State and USAID hire workers based on their abilities and make sure their workforce reflects the diverse backgrounds of all American people they represent around the world. In nineteen ninety, Congress changed the State Department Basic Authorities Act of nineteen fifty six to allow the Secretary of State to give money to colleges or students. This money helps increase knowledge of and interest in working for the Foreign Service, with special focus on minority students. The goal is to make recruiting and keeping workers more inclusive so everyone has equal opportunity and the country can use the strengths of all its citizens. Using these new powers, the Department of State started the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Fellowship in nineteen ninety two, the Charles B. Rangel International Affairs Program in two thousand two, and the William D. Clarke, Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship in two thousand twenty three. These programs help include more Pell grant eligible students and first generation college graduates in the Foreign Service. Most current fellows received Pell grants to help pay for college. The Charles B. Rangel International Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program and the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Fellowship Program are the Department of State's main programs for recruiting top talent. These programs are based on merit and financial need and are highly competitive, with less than five percent of applicants accepted each year. All fellows must pass the same tough selection, hiring, and security clearance process as all other Foreign Service members. Research shows that having a workforce that represents all of America helps improve national security. This happens because diverse workers provide more perspectives, experiences, and cultural understanding. This leads to better threat identification, more creative solutions, and stronger diplomatic relationships around the world. International affairs fellowships that promote hiring people from historically excluded groups and those with financial need are smart investments. The Charles B. Rangel International Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program, the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Fellowship Program, the William D. Clarke, Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship, and the Donald M. Payne International Development Fellowship Program are vital for building a strong, merit based, capable, and diverse national security workforce. Congress has authorized each of these fellowship programs with support from both political parties. They recognize these programs are important for expanding merit based and need based recruiting from a wide geographic and economically diverse talent pool. This includes recruiting from all fifty states and more than five hundred colleges and universities. Historically Black Colleges and Universities, Hispanic serving institutions, other minority serving institutions, and other colleges including community colleges and trade schools serve populations historically excluded from the Department of State or USAID. These schools prepare the next generation of international affairs professionals with the core skills needed to meet America's global diplomatic and development needs. The Secretary of State and Administrator of USAID are required by law to talk with Congress before taking steps to change these programs.

sections.title: Resolved Clauses
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the importance of efforts to recruit, hire, and keep employees from the broadest talent pool for United States foreign affairs agencies. This helps the United States be globally competitive and ensures that our diplomatic and development agencies remain the best in the world. The House reaffirms that the Charles B. Rangel Graduate Fellowship Program, Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program, William D. Clarke Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship, and Donald M. Payne International Development Graduate Fellowship Program are programs required by law. Congress created these programs with support from both political parties to address recognized problems of exclusion for women and racial and ethnic minority groups as well as economically disadvantaged and rural populations that have affected the Department of State and United States Agency for International Development for decades. The House emphasizes the importance of international affairs fellowships and similar career entry programs to United States national security and foreign policy. The House recognizes the substantial United States taxpayer investment in ensuring the Department of State and United States Agency for International Development can recruit top talent from across the country, provide them with critical training, and strengthen America's development and diplomatic capabilities. These efforts are harmed by attempts to dismantle these programs, which wastes taxpayer resources and weakens national security.
